OIIX A4458/24.explanation: This is an important message for pilots flying between Tehran and Kabul airspaces. If you're flying from Kabul into Tehran, you must contact Tehran air traffic control at least 5 minutes before crossing the border, using specific radio frequencies for your route. However, if you're flying from Tehran into Kabul, you do so at your own risk. Air traffic services will only be provided up to the border with Kabul airspace, meaning you're on your own once you enter Kabul's airspace.

OIIX A4459/24.explanation: This is a warning from Iran to its airlines about flying in, out of, or over Afghanistan. It says that the airspace over Afghanistan is dangerous because there aren't proper air traffic control services, there's a risk from militant groups, and it's hard to deal with emergencies. Iranian airlines are told to be very careful and consider these serious risks when planning any flights in that area.

OIIX A4460/24.explanation: This is an official warning from Iran's aviation authority to Iranian airlines and pilots. It tells them there's a serious security risk when flying over certain areas in and around Ukraine, Rostov (Russia), and Moldova. The danger comes from increased military activity, anti-aircraft weapons, and the potential for mistakes or misunderstandings due to the ongoing conflict in Ukraine. Pilots are advised to carefully consider these risks when planning flights in these regions.

OIIX A4461/24.explanation: This is an urgent safety warning for all aircraft operators. They are strongly advised not to fly into the airspace over Sudan (known as Khartoum FIR) because it is currently very dangerous. There's a high risk from anti-aircraft weapons and intense military activity. This warning is issued by Iran's aviation authority and will be in effect from November 2024 until February 2025. Pilots should contact the Iran Civil Aviation Authority for more details and check the provided ICAO website for alternative flight routes.

OIIX A0712/26.explanation: An important air route, called N39, in Tehran's airspace (between DEMBA and OBRIX) will be closed for all planes flying through that area. This closure will last from February 28 to May 29, 2026. Planes flying west will need to use an alternative route through RADAL, IMKER, and ULDUS, flying no lower than 27,500 feet. Planes flying east will need to use an alternative route through ULDUS, ALKUP, IMLIM, OXADU, and OBRIX, flying no lower than 28,500 feet.

OIIX A0713/26.explanation: An air highway (called G208/L125) in the airspace over Tehran, Iran, is temporarily closed for planes that are just passing through the area. If a pilot planned to use this specific route, they now need to fly a different, specified path (via ROVAD direct to IKA). However, planes arriving at Tehran's main airports (Imam Khomeini, Mehrabad, and Payam) can still use a specific entry point (RADAL). This change is in effect from February 28 to May 10, 2026.

OIIX A0724/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in the Tehran region. It provides new, additional phone numbers (a direct line and a mobile number) to contact Air Traffic Control.

OIIX A0781/26.explanation: This is an important message for foreign airlines and aircraft. If you are flying into, out of, or through Iranian airspace (Tehran FIR), you must send your flight plans and any updates to a specific address: OIIXIFPS. You also need to follow the detailed procedures mentioned in Iran's aviation manual (AIP ENR 1.11). This rule is in effect from April 20, 2026, to July 19, 2026.

OIIX A0782/26.explanation: This is an important message for pilots flying in the Tehran airspace. Several specific high-frequency radio channels that pilots use to talk to Tehran air traffic information services are currently not working. This affects all types of flights, from the ground up to any altitude, and will last from April 20, 2026, until July 19, 2026. Pilots need to use alternative communication methods or frequencies during this time.

OIIX A0784/26.explanation: This NOTAM announces a new, permanent controlled airspace established south of Tehran, Iran. This new area starts at 19,500 feet and extends upwards indefinitely. At 20,000 feet, it's designated as Class D airspace, meaning pilots must communicate with air traffic control. Above 20,000 feet, it becomes Class A airspace, which is highly controlled and primarily for instrument flights. Tehran Air Traffic Control will manage this area 24 hours a day on frequency 133.400 MHz, using English and Persian. This change is important for pilots flying through this region.

OIIX A0785/26.explanation: This notice tells pilots that the radio frequencies they use to talk to air traffic control in various parts of Iran's airspace are changing. From April 20, 2026, until July 19, 2026, several existing frequencies for different sectors will be replaced by a new frequency, 258.400 MHz. Pilots flying in this area must use the new frequency for communication.

OIIX B0187/26.explanation: This is an update to air traffic rules for domestic flights in the Tehran airspace. It specifies new, mandatory routes that planes must follow when flying between two particular airports (OIII and OIMD) in Iran. These changes are in effect from April 20, 2026, to July 19, 2026.

OIIX A0793/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying within Iran. It changes the specific routes (called SIDs and STARs) that certain domestic flights must follow when traveling between particular airports, like from OIBK to OIMM, or from OISS to OICC, OICK, and OICI. These changes are part of a larger plan to manage air traffic and are detailed in an official aviation document.

OIIX A0863/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots about specific, mandatory flight paths that planes must use when flying to Tehran's Imam Khomeini International Airport (OIMM) from three other domestic airports (OISS, OIBB, and OIYY). It's part of a traffic management plan to ensure planes follow designated routes.

OIIX A0893/26.explanation: This is an official notice for pilots about several 'highways in the sky' (airways) in Iran that will be temporarily closed. These specific routes will be unavailable for aircraft to fly on from May 5, 2026, until August 3, 2026. Pilots will need to plan alternative routes during this time.

OIIX A0912/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in Iran. A specific air route, called Airway A647, between two points named NAZAR and RAGET, has been changed. Previously, aircraft might have only been able to fly in one direction on this segment, but now they can fly in both directions. This is a change to the air traffic rules for that specific 'highway in the sky'.

OIIX B0208/26.explanation: This is a warning for pilots that there will be drone activity in a specific area from May 6, 2026, to June 5, 2026. The drones will be operating from the ground up to 10,000 feet above sea level within the defined coordinates. Pilots should be aware of this activity when flying in that region.

OIIX A0957/26.explanation: A vital navigation beacon, called VOR ANK, which helps aircraft find their way, is currently not working. It operates on frequency 112.700 MHz and will be out of service from May 12, 2026, until August 10, 2026. Once repaired, it will need to be tested by an aircraft in flight to ensure it's working correctly before it can be used again.

OIIX A0916/26.explanation: An area in Iran, specifically designated as OID121, will be restricted for all aircraft traffic from May 13 to June 21, 2026. This restriction will be active daily between 03:30 AM and 07:30 PM UTC, covering altitudes from the ground up to 30,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX B0209/26.explanation: There will be gun firing in a specific area from May 13 to June 21, 2026, daily between 03:30 UTC and 18:30 UTC. This activity will take place from the ground up to 13,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should be aware of this danger area.

OIIX A0917/26.explanation: There will be gun firing exercises in a specific area in Iran from May 13 to June 21, 2026. These exercises will happen daily between 03:30 and 19:30 UTC. The affected airspace is from the ground up to 20,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should be aware of this military activity.

OIIX A0973/26.explanation: This is an update to the 'Traffic Orientation Scheme' for domestic flights within the Tehran airspace. It changes specific flight paths (routes for taking off and landing) between various airports in Iran. Think of it as updating the 'road map' for planes flying between these cities.

OIIX A0974/26.explanation: This is an official aviation notice announcing changes to the specific flight paths (routes) that aircraft must follow when flying into or out of major airports in Iran (Tehran, Shiraz, Mashhad) and when traveling to or from neighboring airspaces like Muscat (Oman) and Baghdad (Iraq). It updates the 'Traffic Orientation Scheme' for international arrivals and departures, essentially changing the designated 'roads in the sky' for these regions.

OIIX A0933/26.explanation: An area called OID125 in the Tehran region will be a restricted airspace daily from May 14 to June 21, 2026, between 4:30 AM and 6:30 PM UTC. Aircraft will not be allowed to fly through this area from the ground up to 18,000 feet during those times. Pilots need to check their maps for more details.

OIIX A0931/26.explanation: Attention pilots! There will be gun firing exercises in a specific area within Iran's airspace. These exercises will take place every Saturday and Thursday between 11:30 AM and 2:30 PM UTC, starting from May 14, 2026, and ending on June 20, 2026. The affected area extends from the ground up to 18,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should be aware of this activity and avoid the specified region during these times for safety.

OIIX A0978/26.explanation: For a specific air route (M434/Z740) between two points (ASLAX and NOLTO), the lowest altitude aircraft are allowed to fly has been temporarily increased to 19,000 feet. This change is active on Saturdays and Thursdays during specific hours, from May 14 to June 20, 2026.

OIIX B0210/26.explanation: This is a warning for pilots about military gun firing exercises. These exercises will happen daily from May 22, 2026, to June 21, 2026, between 4:30 AM and 11:30 AM UTC. The firing will take place in a circular area with a 5-kilometer radius, centered near 38°10'N 47°15'E, from the ground up to 9,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should avoid this area during these times.

OIIX A1020/26.explanation: The airspace over Tehran has partially reopened. The western part is still closed for planes flying through. The eastern part is open, but planes must follow strict rules: they need to contact air traffic control at specific times, have a working collision avoidance system, and might be kept further apart. Radar services are available above a certain altitude.

OIIX A1021/26.explanation: This is a warning for all flights. It states that a dangerous area, possibly a conflict zone, might expand into international airspace over the Persian Gulf and part of the Oman Sea. This is due to security concerns, and flights should be cautious in that region.

OIIX A1022/26.explanation: Many airports in the western part of Iran are currently closed. A specific list of airports remains open, but only from sunrise to sunset. All previously approved flight permissions are cancelled, and airlines must request new permission from the Iranian aviation authority for any civil passenger flights.

OIIX A1023/26.explanation: Iran's air traffic control has announced that all previous rules for planes flying through their airspace are temporarily stopped. Instead, new specific routes must be used for planes flying above 28,500 feet, especially for those traveling between Oman and Pakistan. This change is happening during a 'recovery period,' suggesting a significant event or situation is being managed.

OIIX A1024/26.explanation: This is an important update for pilots flying over Iran (Tehran airspace). All previous rules for planes flying above 28,500 feet are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes must now be used for flights going between Pakistan and Afghanistan, and vice-versa. This change is happening during a 'recovery period,' which suggests a significant event has occurred requiring these new flight paths.

OIIX A1025/26.explanation: Iran has temporarily changed the rules for planes flying high over its airspace (above 28,500 feet). They've stopped all the old rules for these flights and put new, specific routes in place. This is happening during a 'recovery period,' which suggests there was a previous event or situation that required these changes. Planes flying between Pakistan and Afghanistan through Iranian airspace must now follow these new, specific routes.

OIIX A1026/26.explanation: This is an updated aviation notice for pilots flying over Iran (Tehran airspace). It states that all previous rules for planes flying over Iran are temporarily stopped. New, specific flight paths must now be used for planes flying above 28,500 feet, especially when traveling between Afghanistan (Kabul airspace) and Tajikistan (Dushanbe airspace). This change is in effect during what is called a 'recovery period,' suggesting things are returning to normal after a previous event.

OIIX A1027/26.explanation: This is an update for airplanes flying through the Tehran airspace. It changes the rules for planes flying high above 28,500 feet. The old rules for flying through are temporarily stopped, and new specific routes have been set up for planes traveling between Kabul and Baku airspaces during a recovery period.

OIIX A1028/26.explanation: This is an aviation alert from Iran (Tehran region). It announces that all previous rules for planes flying over Iran are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes are now required for planes flying above 28,500 feet, especially for flights between Oman and Azerbaijan. There's also a special route for planes heading to the United Arab Emirates. These changes are in effect during what's called a 'recovery period,' suggesting there was a previous event that disrupted air travel.

OIIX A1029/26.explanation: This is an urgent update for pilots flying through Iranian airspace. All previous rules for planes flying over Iran are temporarily stopped. New, specific routes are now mandatory for planes flying above 28,500 feet, especially for those traveling between Muscat (Oman) and Tashkent (Uzbekistan), and vice-versa. These changes are in effect during a 'recovery period,' meaning pilots must strictly follow these new designated paths instead of the old ones.

OIIX A1030/26.explanation: This is an important update for pilots flying over Iran (Tehran airspace) at high altitudes (above 28,500 feet). It cancels all previous rules for flying through this airspace and sets new, specific routes that planes must follow. These changes are in effect during a 'recovery period,' which suggests there was a previous event or disruption that made these new routes necessary. There are different routes depending on whether planes are flying from Muscat (Oman) to Karachi (Pakistan) or vice-versa, with a special route for planes landing in the United Arab Emirates.

OIIX A1031/26.explanation: This is an update for international flights flying into or out of Iran's airspace. It confirms that the specific points where air traffic control hands over responsibility between Iran's airspace and the airspaces of Turkey, Armenia, and Azerbaijan are open and available for use.

OIIX A1032/26.explanation: This is an update for pilots flying in the Tehran airspace. It means that all established air routes can now be navigated using a specific type of satellite-based navigation system called RNAV 5, starting from the lowest safe flying altitude. This is a standard update to navigation procedures.

OIIX A1036/26.explanation: The main radar system used by air traffic controllers in Tehran (Mehrabad) is currently out of order. This means that pilots flying in certain parts of the airspace around Tehran might not receive radar services, making it harder for controllers to track and guide aircraft. This situation will last from May 25, 2026, until June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1038/26.explanation: The main radar system at Tehran's Imam Khomeini Airport is currently not working. This means that air traffic controllers might not have radar information for planes flying in certain areas (sectors 1, 2, and 7) of the airspace. As a result, radar services in these specific areas may not be available to pilots. This situation is expected to last from May 25, 2026, until June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1037/26.explanation: This is an administrative notice for pilots and air traffic controllers in the Tehran flight region. It provides a comprehensive list of all currently active aviation notices (NOTAMs), updates to aeronautical information publications (AIP Amendments), and other important circulars (AIP SUP and AIC) that they need to be aware of. It also informs them that no new major updates to the flight information publications will be released on May 14, 2026, and June 11, 2026.

OIIX B0249/26.explanation: This is a routine update for pilots and aviation staff in the Tehran region. It's like a table of contents, listing all the important active notices (NOTAMs), aviation publications (AIP Amendments, Supplements), and information circulars that they need to be aware of. It also mentions that no new major aviation updates will be released on May 14 and June 11, 2026.

OIIX A1039/26.explanation: A main radar system in Iran (called Kushk MSSR) is broken and will not be working until June 29th. This means air traffic controllers in certain parts of Iran's airspace, specifically in sectors 1, 2, and 7, will not have radar information to track planes. This will affect flights that rely on instruments and may limit air traffic control services in these areas.

OIIX A1040/26.explanation: The radar system at Kish Island is currently broken and will be out of service until late June. This means air traffic controllers in the Tehran area will not have radar information for planes flying in certain parts of the airspace (specifically sectors 4 and 5). Pilots and controllers will need to use other methods to manage air traffic, which could lead to some operational changes or delays.

OIIX A1041/26.explanation: The radar system in Esfahan (Isfahan) is currently broken and will be out of service until June 29, 2026. This means that air traffic controllers might not be able to see planes on their radar screens in certain parts of the airspace (specifically sectors 2, 3, 6 West, and 7). Pilots and air traffic control will need to be aware of this reduced radar coverage.

OIIX A1042/26.explanation: A main radar system in Iran (called LAR MSSR) is currently broken and not working. This means that air traffic controllers might not be able to see planes on their radar screens in certain areas, especially in a specific air traffic control sector (Sector 4, on frequency 133.400 MHz). This situation will last from May 25, 2026, until June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1043/26.explanation: A radar system in Shiraz, Iran, is currently broken and will be out of service until June 29, 2026. This means that air traffic controllers will not have radar information for planes flying long distances in that area. Additionally, radar services in two specific air traffic control sectors (Sector 3 and Sector 4) might also be unavailable. Pilots and air traffic controllers will need to be aware of this to ensure safe flight operations.

OIIX A1044/26.explanation: A radar system in the Ahwaz region is currently out of order. This means air traffic controllers might not be able to see aircraft on their radar screens in certain parts of the airspace, specifically in two control sectors. This affects planes flying long distances and is expected to last from May 25 to June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1045/26.explanation: The radar system in Tabriz, Iran, is currently broken and out of service. This means that air traffic controllers will not have radar information for planes flying in certain parts of the airspace, particularly in a specific control sector. Pilots flying in these areas might not receive radar services. This issue is expected to last from May 25 to June 29, 2026.

OIIX A1046/26.explanation: Two specific points in the sky, named RAGET and PAXAT, are now available for air traffic controllers to hand over flights between Iran's and Iraq's airspaces. This is only for flights that are either arriving at or departing from airports within Iran, and it's available daily from 2:30 AM to 2:30 PM UTC.

OIIX A1047/26.explanation: This is an important update for flights in the eastern part of the Tehran region. Most airports there will now only be open during the day. A few specific airports will remain open 24 hours, and two others will have specific operating hours. All old flight approvals are no longer valid, and new ones must be requested from the Iranian aviation authorities.

OIIX B0250/26.explanation: This is a temporary rule for flying in Iran. Most private and visual flights (where pilots fly by looking outside) are stopped. However, flights around specific airports (OIMG and OIMS), flights for emergencies (like hospital, search and rescue), government, or military operations are still allowed. If you want to fly visually for other reasons, you need special permission. Flights that normally use instruments but have to switch to visual rules just for landing or takeoff at small airports are not affected by this rule.

OIIX A1049/26.explanation: For about a month, any aircraft flying in the western part of the airspace managed by Tehran (Iran) must have a specific type of modern tracking and broadcasting equipment called ADS-B. This equipment helps air traffic controllers see the aircraft more clearly and accurately. If an aircraft doesn't have this equipment, it cannot fly in that specific area.

OIIX A1056/26.explanation: This NOTAM announces that a specific restricted airspace in Iran, known as 'OIR42 UROMIYEH', has been permanently removed. Pilots no longer need to avoid this area, and aviation maps and publications should be updated to reflect this change.

OIIX A1006/26.explanation: A specific area in the sky near Tehran, called OID84, is now off-limits for aircraft. This restriction is active daily from May 28 to July 22, 2026, between 3:30 AM and 7:30 PM UTC, from the ground up to 13,000 feet. Pilots need to avoid this area.

OIIX A1008/26.explanation: This is an aviation alert for pilots flying in the Iran region. It means that a specific area, identified as OID126, will be restricted for aircraft. This restriction will be in effect daily from May 28 to July 22, 2026, between 03:30 AM and 04:30 PM Universal Coordinated Time (UTC). During these times, no aircraft will be allowed to fly from the ground up to 14,000 feet above sea level within this designated area. Pilots need to be aware of this restricted zone to avoid it.

OIIX B0248/26.explanation: This is a warning for pilots that there will be gun firing in a specific area in Iran. The firing will take place daily from May 28, 2026, to July 22, 2026, between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM local time. Aircraft should avoid this area from the ground up to 15,000 feet above sea level during these times.

OIIX A1053/26.explanation: An area in the Tehran region, identified as OID122, will be restricted for flights. This means aircraft might not be allowed to fly through this specific airspace. This restriction will be in effect from May 30 to July 22, 2026, daily between 02:30 AM and 08:30 PM UTC, from the ground up to 16,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX A1054/26.explanation: An area identified as 'OID123' within the Tehran flight region will be active daily from May 30 to July 22, 2026, between 02:30 AM and 08:30 PM UTC. This means that flights from the ground up to 18,000 feet above sea level will be restricted or warned to avoid this specific zone during those times.

OIIX A1055/26.explanation: An area of airspace in Iran, specifically designated as OID128, is now a restricted zone. This means aircraft are not allowed to fly through it. This restriction is in effect daily from May 30th to July 22nd, between 02:30 AM and 04:30 PM UTC, from the ground up to 15,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX B0251/26.explanation: Attention all pilots and air travelers: There will be gun firing exercises taking place in a specific area. This will happen daily from 03:30 AM to 06:30 PM UTC, starting on May 30, 2026, and continuing until July 22, 2026. The affected area is from the ground up to 14,000 feet above sea level. Please be aware of this activity if you plan to fly in or near this region.

OIIX B0252/26.explanation: Attention all aircraft! There will be gun firing in a specific area within Iran's airspace. This will happen daily from May 30, 2026, until July 22, 2026, between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM UTC. The affected area is from the ground up to 14,000 feet above sea level. Pilots should be aware of this activity and avoid the specified coordinates during these times for safety.

OIIX A1058/26.explanation: Attention pilots! There will be gun firing exercises taking place in a specific area within Iran's airspace (OIIX FIR). These exercises will occur daily from May 26th to July 22nd, between 03:30 AM and 08:30 PM UTC. The activity will extend from the ground up to 20,000 feet above sea level. Pilots flying in this region should be aware of this military activity.

OIIX B0255/26.explanation: Attention pilots and the public: There will be gun firing in a specific area near Mashhad, Iran. This activity will take place daily between 03:30 AM and 06:30 PM local time, starting from May 30, 2026, and continuing until July 22, 2026. The affected area extends from the ground up to 11,000 feet above sea level. Aircraft should be aware and avoid this region during these times.

OIIX B0254/26.explanation: A specific area in the sky over Iran, known as OID124, is now a restricted zone. This means aircraft are not allowed to fly there. This restriction is in effect daily from May 30th to July 22nd, 2026, between 4:30 AM and 8:30 PM UTC. The restricted area extends from the ground up to 13,000 feet above sea level.

OIIX A0982/26.explanation: This is a notice for pilots and aviation personnel in the Iran region. It means that on June 11, 2026, there will be no new updates to the official aviation documents, like maps and procedures, even though it's a date when such updates are usually expected.

KICZ A0016/20.explanation: The U.S. government is warning American airlines, pilots, and aircraft operators to be very careful when flying over the Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. This is due to ongoing military activity and political tensions in the region. They should review security information, avoid routes close to Iranian airspace to prevent being mistaken for something else by air defense systems, and be prepared for potential GPS and communication jamming. They must also report any safety or security incidents. A separate, stricter warning that bans U.S. flights over Iranian airspace is still in effect.

KICZ A0011/24.explanation: The United States government has issued a new rule (SFAR 117) that bans certain US flights from flying in the airspace over Tehran, Iran. This ban started on October 3, 2024, and is expected to last until October 31, 2027. All pilots and airlines are required to read the full details of this rule.
